content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
by 19702.1(a)(4)

August 11, 1993

Honorable Gloria Exline
Vice Mayor
City of Vallejo
159 Greenfield Avenue
Vallejo, CA 94591

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-286

Dear Vice Mayor Exline:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as Vice Mayor of the City of
Vallejo and a member of the Governing Board of the Vallejo
Sanitation and Flood Control District pursuant to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)

Finally, please note that the Commission's advice is limited
to the provisions of the Political Reform Act. (Sections 81000-
91015.) The Commission has no jurisdiction over other laws that
may apply to your facts, such as Section 1090 or the doctrine of
incompatible offices. If you wish advice with respect to these
areas of the law, you should contact the Attorney General's
Office.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-286
Page 2


QUESTIONS

1. Are the Vallejo Sanitation and Flood Control District
Certificates of Participation government bonds exempt from
disclosure under the Act?

2. Is your investment in the district the basis for a
conflict of interest under the Act?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act exempts bonds and other debt instruments issued
by governments or government agencies from the definitions of both
"investment" and "income." Since the Vallejo Sanitation and Flood
Control District is a public entity and the certificates of
participation are certificates issued to show a proportionate
interest in the debt of the entity, they are not disclosable under
the Act.

2. The Act prohibits public officials from participating in
decisions that will materially affect their own assets and income.
However, since the interests on the certificates are fixed at the
time of purchase and they are insured against possible default, it
does not appear reasonably foreseeable that any decision affecting
the district will materially affect the certificates. Thus, they
would not result in a conflict of interest.

FACTS

You are the Vice Mayor of the Vallejo City Council and also a
member of the governing board of the Vallejo Sanitation and Flood
Control District (the "district"), a public entity created by
special legislative statute.

Recently, the district issued certificates of participation
to raise revenue to refinance a pre-existing debt and to fund
future projects in the district's Master Plan. You expect that
the principal source of revenue for installment payments to retire
these certificates (and to fund all operation and Maintenance
generally) are user fees which are charged to users of the
district's sanitation and storm drainage facilities. User fees
are periodically reviewed to determine whether they must be raised
or lowered to meet costs of operation and maintenance, a component
part of which will be to retire existing indebtedness.

According to the official statement regarding the
certificates you submitted with your request and our telephone
conversation of August 3, 1993, the interest on the investment is
fixed at the time of purchase. In addition, payment on the
certificates is fully insured in the event of default by the
district.

Our File No. A-93-286
Page 3


You stated that upon issuance, you invested in $10,000 worth
of the certificates of participation. You have asked whether the
purchase will result in any disclosure or disqualification
obligations under the Act.

ANALYSIS

1. Economic Interests

The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act generally do
not prohibit a public official's private conduct. Thus, the Act
does not prohibit you from purchasing the district's certificates
of participation.

Section 87100 of the Act does prohibit any public official
from making, participating in making, or otherwise using the
official's position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides:

An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * *

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institu-
tion in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 87103(a) and (d).

As Vice Mayor of the City of Vallejo and a member of the
Governing Board of the Vallejo Sanitation and Flood Control
District, you are a public official under the Act.² Thus, you are


2 A "public official" is defined in Section 82048 and Regulation
18700 as every natural person who is a member, officer, employee,
or consultant of a state or local government agency.

Our File No. A-93-286
Page 4


prohibited from participating in any decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic
interests.

     A.  The Vallejo Sanitation and Flood Control District

     According to your facts, you have purchased certificates of
participation from the district. If these certificates
constituted an "investment" in a "business entity," you would have
an economic interest in decisions that affect the district.

     However, Section 82034 defines "investment" as any financial
interest in or security issued by a "business entity," but
excludes any bond or debt instrument issued by a government
agency.³ Moreover, Section 82005 defines a "business entity" as
any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but
not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business
trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association. This
definition would not include governmental agencies. (Fatland
Advice Letter, No. I-89-419.) Thus, you would not have an
interest in the district by virtue of the certificates of
participation under Section 87103(a).⁴

     Additionally, the definition of "income" in Section 82030
excludes "interest, dividends, or premiums on a time or demand
deposit in a financial institution, shares in a credit union or
any insurance policy, payments received under any insurance
policy, or any bond or other debt instrument issued by any
government or government agency." (Section 82030(b)(4).)
Consequently, you would not have an interest in the district by
virtue of interest derived from the certificates of participation
for purposes of disclosure or disqualification.⁵

     B.  Direct Effects on You or Your Immediate Family

     Section 87103 and Regulation 18702.1(a)(4) provide that
decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material

_____________

     ³ A "certificate of participation" is defined in Black's Law
Dictionary (1979) as "A certificate issued instead of shares of
stock to show a proportionate interest in an unincorporated
business or in the ownership of debt of a corporation."

     ⁴ Similarly, Section 87103(d) only applies to an official's
business position with a business entity, which the Vallejo
Sanitation and Flood Control District is not.

     ⁵ Section 82030 also exempts salary and reimbursement for
expenses or per diem received from a state, local, or federal
government agency, such as the district and the City of Vallejo.
(Section 82030(b)(2).)

Our File No. A-93-286
Page 5


financial effect on the official's personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities are also potentially disqualifying
financial interests.

     Consequently, you may not make, participate in making or in
any way attempt to use your official position to influence a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the value of your
certificates of participation or the interest you receive from the
certificates.

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.  An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.  Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

     Thus, for example, it is foreseeable that you will be
materially affected if a decision affects the repayment of the
certificates.  However, according to the official statement
pertaining to the certificates of participation and our telephone
conversation of August 3, 1993, the interest rate on the
certificates is fixed at the time of purchase and the repayment of
the certificates is fully insured.  Thus, it is not foreseeable
that your official decisions will financially affect you.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Wayne Ordos
                                        Executive Director

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division


_________________________
6  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

Certificates of Participation government bonds exempt from
disclosure under the Act?
2.
Is your investment in the district the basis for a
conflict of interest under the Act?

CONCLUSION: 1.
The Act exempts bonds and other debt instruments issued
by governments or government agencies from the definitions of both
“investment” and “income.”
Since the Vallejo Sanitation and Flood
Control District is a public entity and the certificates of
participation are certificates issued to show a proportionate
interest in the debt of the entity, they are not disclosable under
the Act.
2.
The Act prohibits public officials from participating in
decisions that will materially affect their own assets and income.
However, since the interests on the certificates are fixed at the
time of purchase and the.y are insured against possible default, it
does not appear reasonably foreseeable that any decision affecting
the district will materially affect the certificates.
Thus,. they
would not result in a conflict of interest.
